coredata.dc:title: Folic acid functionalized molybdenum oxide quantum dots for the detection of Cu2+ ion and alkaline phosphatase via fluorescence turn off–on mechanism
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Kateshiya, Mehul R.
Malek, Naved I.
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
coredata.dc:description: The assay of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) plays a key role in the diagnosis of various diseases. Herein, folic acid functionalized molybdenum oxide quantum dots (FA-MoOx QDs) are explored as fluorescence “turn- off and on” probes for assaying of Cu2+ ion and ALP, respectively. This fluorescence sensing strategy was based on the quenching of emission peak of FA-MoOx QDs at 445 nm by Cu2+ ion, followed by restoring of emission peak selectively with ALP. Based on the quenching and restoring of FA-MoOx QDs emission intensity, quantitative assay was developed for the detection of Cu2+ ion (0.20 – 500 µM) and ALP (0.06 – 150 U/L) with detection limits of 29 nM and 0.026 U/L, respectively. The developed FA-MoOx QDs-based fluorescence “turn- off and on” strategy exhibited satisfactory results for assaying of ALP in biofluids.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FA-MoOx QDs
Cu2+ ion
Alkaline phosphatase
Biofluids and fluorescence assay

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assaying of Cu2+ ion and ALP via fluorescence “turn- off and on” mechanims
Fluorescence assay of ALP in serum and urine samples
Fluorescence “turn- off and on” assays of Cu2+ ion and ALP
Selectivity of FA-MoOx QDs-based fluorescence assay
Fluorescence assay of ALP in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Science and Technology, Government of India
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India (EMR/2016/002621/IPC). MK sincerely acknowledges the Director, SVNIT, Surat, India for providing the doctoral fellowship to carry this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–visible absorption, emission (445 nm) and excitation (360 nm) spectra of FA-MoOx QDs (Inset: Image of FA-MoOx QDs under UV lamp at 365 nm and daylight).

HR-TEM images of (a) lattice fringes of FA-MoOx QDs at 2 nm, (b) at 5 nm scale bar, (c) FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion, (d) FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion with ALP. Histograms are drawn at a 5 nm scale bar for (e) FA-MoOx QDs, (f) FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion and (g) FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion-ALP.
XPS spectra of FA-MoOx QDs, (a) all elements (Mo, C, N, and O), (b) Mo 3d and (c) N 1 s.

Fluorescence emission spectra of FA-MoOx QDs in the presence (a) divalent metal ions (Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, and Hg2+, 1 mM), (b) photographic image of (1) FA-MoOx QDs solution under UV lamp at 365 nm upon the addition of metal ions of (2) Cu2+, (3) Pb2+, (4) Mn2+, (5) Zn2+, (6) Co2+, (7) Ni2+ and (8) Hg2+. Emission spectra of FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion with addition of (c) various biomolecules (ALP (200 U/L), ATP, AMP, cysteine, PPi, BSA, trypsin, pepsin and glutathione, 500 μΜ) and (d) photograph image of (1) FA-MoOx QDs solution under UV lamp at 365 nm upon the addition of biomolecules of (2) FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion, (3) ALP, (4) ATP, (5) AMP, (6) cysteine, (7) PPi, (8) BSA, (9) trypsin, (10) pepsin and (11) glutathione.

Fluorescence spectral responses of FA-MoOx QDs upon addition of (a) Cu2+ (0.2–500 µM). Inset: Fluorescence changes in FA-MoOx QDs solution with different amounts of Cu2+ (0.2–500 µM) under UV lamp at 365 nm. (b) Emission spectral changes of FA-MoOx QDs by the introduction of ALP (0.06–150 U/L). Inset: Fluorescence changes in FA-MoOx QDs solution by the introduction of different amounts of ALP (0.06–150 U/L) under UV lamp at 365 nm.
Investigation of FA-MoOx QDs selectivity towards (a) Cu2+ion and (b) ALP in the presence of interfering chemical substances.
Synthesis of FA-MoOx QDs via the surface modification of MoOx nanoparticles with FA.
Fluorescence “turn- off and on” assays of Cu2+ ion and ALP using FA-MoOx QDs as a probe.
Analytical performance of FA-MoOx QDs-based fluorescence assay of ALP compared with reported methods in the literature.

head.ce:title: Folic acid functionalized molybdenum oxide quantum dots for the detection of Cu2+ ion and alkaline phosphatase via fluorescence turn off–on mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007, Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallbhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007, Gujarat, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The assay of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) plays a key role in the diagnosis of various diseases. Herein, folic acid functionalized molybdenum oxide quantum dots (FA-MoOx QDs) are explored as fluorescence “turn- off and on” probes for assaying of Cu2+ ion and ALP, respectively. This fluorescence sensing strategy was based on the quenching of emission peak of FA-MoOx QDs at 445 nm by Cu2+ ion, followed by restoring of emission peak selectively with ALP. Based on the quenching and restoring of FA-MoOx QDs emission intensity, quantitative assay was developed for the detection of Cu2+ ion (0.20 – 500 µM) and ALP (0.06 – 150 U/L) with detection limits of 29 nM and 0.026 U/L, respectively. The developed FA-MoOx QDs-based fluorescence “turn- off and on” strategy exhibited satisfactory results for assaying of ALP in biofluids.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantitative assay of trace level metal ions and biomarkers has become more importance in branches of sciences including food, environmental and biomedical sciences [1–6]. In view of this, the introduction of novel materials into analytical method development has received extensive attention due to their significancy in resolving problems (rapidity, selectivity and sensitivity) in analytical chemistry [7–11]. Recent years, molybdenum oxide (MoOx, 2 ≤ x ≤ 3) quantum dots (QDs) have shown remarkable permormance in various fields of science including analytical chemistry and biomedical sciences [1,12]. The composition and structure of MoOx play key role in tuning their performance for various applications [13–16]. To support this, the difference in the oxidation states of Mo in MoOx (Mo4+ and Mo6+) showed significant variations in optical and physico-chemical properties [17]. Importantly, the variations in the MoOx structures offer to explore as promising materials in catalytic and sensing applications [1]. In order to produce MoOx nanomaterials with control size and morphology, multiple synthetic approaches such as hydrothermal, solvothermal, chemical vapor deposition and template assisted method have been used for the fabrication of MoOx nanomaterials with diverse dimensions and structural properties, exhibiting promising applications in gas sensing, ion battery and drug delivery [18]. Noticeably, properties and electronic structures of MoOx nanomaterials are effectively tuned by either doping of metal ions or surface modificaitons with inorganic/organic ligands [1]. These reports inspired us to fabricate fluorescent MoOx QDs via simple reaction and their analytical applications in sensing of target molecules via fluorescence “turn- off and on” strategies.

Copper is one of the element essential for various biochemical pathways in living systems [19]. Importantly, it has been recognized as a bioelement due to its key roles in structure and properties of enzymes [20]. The deficit or excess copper causes several diseases like Alzheimer’s and Parkinsons [21,22]. In view of this, several analytical techniques such as colorimetry [23], atomic absorption spectroscopy [24], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry [25] have been used for the detection of Cu2+ ion. However, these techniques are expensive, and requires tedious sample preparation, which limits their use for rapid and real-time detection of Cu2+ ion. Importantly, the permissible limit of Cu2+ ion is 1.3 ppm (∼20 μM) in drinking water, which has been set by the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [26]. Recently, a review has been summarized the potential applications of pyrene functional groups-based moieties as chemosensors for the detection of Cu2+ ion [26]. Althrough these organic molecules based chemical sensors exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity towards Cu2+ ion, they required specific organic molecules and multiple reaction steps for the preparation of chemical sensors. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is a key enzyme that promotes the dephosphorylation in various cells, thereby removing phosphate group from biomolecules (nucleic acids, proteins and carbohydrates) [27]. It is widely distributed in various living organisms (intestine, bone, liver, placenta, and kidney) [28–29]. The levels of ALP are in the range of 20 – 140 U/L in the normal serum [30] and of > 500 U/L in children and pregnant women, demonstrating ALP is a significant analyte in biofluids, which confirms that ALP could be used as a biomarker for the diagnosis of various diseases. To date, numorous analytical strategies including colorimetry [31], chromatography [32], chemiluminescence [33], electrochemical [34], fluorescence [35] and surface-enhanced resonance Raman scattering [36] have been utilized for assaying of ALP in various biofluids and tissues. Among these strategies, fluorescence assay exhibited several analytical merits including rapidity, inexpensive, and sensitivity when compared with other bioanalytical tools. For example, several fluorescent nature materials such as metal nanocluster [37–38], QDs [39–45], organic dyes [46–50] and coordination polymers [51–52] have found to be promising probes for assaying of ALP in various biological samples. Unfortunately, the developed probes showed some limitations such as poor solubility (organic probes) and multi-step synthetic protocols, which significantly limit their wider applications in assaying of ALP in clinical samples. Thus, a simple fluorescence probe is essentially required to detect Cu2+ ion and APL simultaneously in biofluids, which could be used as a rapid analytical strategy for the detection of ALP in biofluids.

Herein, the synthesis of folic acid functionalized MoOx QDs (FA-MoOx QDs) for fluorescence “turn- off and on” assay of Cu2+ ion and ALP (Schemes 1-2
) was discussed. The as-prepared FA-MoOx QDs (where × = 2 ≤ x ≤ 3) showed an intesnse emission intensity at 445 nm, however it was completely quenched by the addition of Cu2+ ion. Interestingly, the quenched emission peak of FA-MoOx QDs was completely restored by the introduction of ALP, favoring to establish a simple analytical strategy. Based on this, a fluorescence assay was developed for the detection of Cu2+ ion and ALP using FA-MoOx QDs as a probe. To illustrate wider applicability of the method, FA-MoOx QDs-based fluorescence “turn- off and on” assay was used for the detection of Cu2+ ion and ALP, showing higher sensitivity, which suggests that the developed method could be used as a simple analytical tool for the detection of ALP as a biomarker for various deseases. The developed fluorescent sensor was successful demonstrated for assaying of Cu2+ and ALP in biofluids, signifying the promising analytical applications of FA-MoOx QDs as a probe in environmental and biomedical sciences.

ALP, Adenosine monophosphate (AMP), Adenosine triphosphate (ATP), cysteine, glutathione, lysozyme, ammonium heptamolybdate tetrahydrate (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, folic acid and all metal salts were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Hexamethylenetetramine (HMT) was obtained from SRL, Pvt, Ltd, India.

Spectral characteristics (absorption and emission spectra) of samples were obtained through a Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics, USA) and Cary Eclipse fluorescence (Agilent Technologies, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific) was used for the investigation of elemental oxidation states in FA-MoOx QDs.

According to the reported method [53], bare MoOx nanoparticles were prepared by using 1.75 g of (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O in 50 mL and 3.0 g of HMT in 25 mL. Both the solutions were taken into a reaction flask and then heated at 90 °C for 1 h. The obtained white precipitate was dried at 70 °C and then used for further functionalization. The surface of MoOx nanoparticles was functionalized with FA via a simple reaction. To be more specific, 50 mg of MoOx nanoparticles was added into a reaction flask that contain 25 mL of water. Then, 30 mM of FA (1.0 mL) was added to the above solution and refluxed for 1.0 h at 90 °C. The formed light yellow color solution was dialyzed against water for 3.0 h at room temperature. The formed FA-MoOx QDs were characterized and studied their analytical application for assaying of Cu2+ ion and ALP.

Quantum yield (QY) of FA-MoOx QDs was measured with the help of quinine sulfate as a reference standard. The following equation was adopted to calculate QY of FA-MoOx QDs.
Q

Where, QYMo- QDs = QY of FA-MoOx QDs, ARf = absorbance of reference, AMo-QDs = absorbance of FA-MoOx QDs, FMo-QDs = fluorescence emission area of FA-MoOx QDs, FRf = fluorescence emission area of reference, ηMo-QDs = fefractive index of FA-MoOx QDs and ηRf = fefractive index of reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assaying of Cu2+ ion and ALP via fluorescence “turn- off and on” mechanims
ce:section.ce:para: For Cu2+ ion assay, 0.50 mL of different concentrations of Cu2+ ion (0.20–500 μM) was added into 1.0 mL of FA-MoOx QDs and the solutions were vortexed for 120 s. Then, the emission spectra of Cu2+ ion-FA-MoOx QDs solutions were measured by exciting them at 360 nm. The fluorescence emission intensities of FA-MoOx QDs without Cu2+ ion (I0) and with Cu2+ ion (I) were used to construct calibration graph for quantitative analysis of Cu2+ ion. For ALP assay, 100 μL of Cu2+ ion (2.0 mM) and 100 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (20 mM, pH 9) were introduced into 1.0 mL of FA-MoOx QDs and then vortexed for 120 s. The fluorescence emission of FA-MoOx QDs was quenched by Cu2+ ion via the formation of Cu2+ ion-FA-MoOx QDs. To restore the fluorescence of FA-MoOx QDs, various concentrations of ALP (0.06 – 150 U/L) were added separately into the above solutions and then vortexed for 120 s. Interestingly, the fluorescence emission intensity of Cu2+ ion-FA-MoOx QDs was progressively increased with increasing amounts of ALP when excited at 360 nm. As a result, fluorescence “turn-off and on” mechanism was established for assaying of Cu2+ ion and ALP using FA-MoOx QDs as a probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of ALP in serum and urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples were collected from Iyers laboratory, Surat, Gujarat, India. The collected serum samples were diluted with Tris-HCl buffer (10-folds). Similarly, urine samples were diluted with water. The samples were spiked with different concentrations of ALP (0.6, 0.7 and 0.8 U/L) and then the amount of ALP was estimated by the described procedure in the above section.

In the present work, MoOx QDs were prepared by the surface modification of bare MoOx nanoparticles with FA (Scheme 1). The absorption, emission, and FT-IR spectra were studied to confirm the formation of FA-MoOx QDs. Similarly, the size, morphology and elemental composition of FA-MoOx QDs were confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and XPS techniques. The optical properties of FA-MoOx QDs were investigated by using UV–Visible and fluorescence spectroscopic techniques. As shown in Figure S1 of Supporting Information, unmodified MoOx QDs did not show any absorbance whereas FA-MoOx QDs showed two absorption bands at 312 and 362 nm. Further, pure FA showed two absorption bands at 322 and 344 nm, however these bands were changed by the modification of MoOx with FA. Similarly, unmodified MoOx nanoparticles did not show any emission peak whereas FA-MoOx QDs showed a strong blue fluorescence emission at 445 nm (Figure S2 of Supporting Information). The amount of FA was investigated on the emission spectra of MoOx QDs (Figure S3a of Supporting Information). The emission peak at 445 nm was progressively increased with increasing concentration of FA from 10 to 30 mM after that the emission intensity was dropdown, suggesting 30 mM of FA was found to be optimum concentration for the synthesis of FA-MoOx QDs. In addition, the influence of reaction time on the fabrication of FA-MoOx QDs was depicted in Figure S3b of Supporting Information. It can be observed that the fluorescence emission spectra of FA-MoOx QDs was steadily increased with increasing reaction time from 0.5 to 2.0 h, after that there was no change in the emission spectra, which confirms that 2.0 h was optimum reaction time. Fig. 1 represents the absorption, excitation and emission spectra of FA-MoOx QDs, showing two absorption bands at 312 and 362 nm, and emission peak at 445 nm upon the excitation of 360 nm. The as-prepared FA-MoOx QDs showed a light transparent yellowish color in daylight and a strong blue color emission appeared when exposed to UV radiation at 365 nm. Importantly, unmodified FA-MoOx QDs did not show any color at daylight, pure folic acid showed a dark yellow color in daylight. These spectral studies demonstrated that the formation of fluorescent MoOx QDs via the surface modification with FA.

From the UV–Visible spectrum of FA-MoOx QDs, two absorption maxima at 312 and 362 nm were noticed. To investigate the maximum emission peak, steady state emission spectra of FA-MoOx QDs were studied at different excitation wavelengths from 340 to 410 nm (Figure S4 of Supporting Information). These emission spectra of FA-MoOx QDs showed that the emission intensity of FA-MoOx QDs was increased with increasing excitation wavelength from 340 to 360 nm and then the emission intensity was decreased with increasing excitation wavelength from 360 to 410 nm. A maximum emission intensity was noticed at 445 nm under excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Thus, the excitation wavelength of 360 nm was selected for the analytical applications of FA-MoOx QDs. The QY of FA-MoOx QDs was found to be 13.54% and exhibited good stability over a period of 90 days. The fluorescence lifetime of FA-MoOx QDs is 13.27 ns (Figure S5 of Supporting Information).

To investigate the existence of functional groups on the surfaces of FA-MoOx QDs, FT-IR spectral characterization is carried out and the spectra are shown in Figure S6a-e of Supporting Information. The FT-IR spectrum of ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate represents the presence of NH4 + ion and the ammonia, where the bands are observed at 1637, 1401, 893, and 655 cm−1 for the deformation modes of δsNH4 +, δasNH4 +, δsNH3 and δasNH3, respectively. The bands at 3208, 3104 and 2804 cm−1 belong to the coordinated NH3 and NH4 + ion. The peaks at 2945 1446 and 1366 cm−1 are assigned to the CH2 group (stretching and bending) and C-N bond (stretching) vibrations, respectively (Figure S6b of Supporting Information). Similarly, the spectrum of unmodified MoOx nanoparticles showed bands at 910 and 962 cm−1, which are corresponded to the stretching of Mo = O bonds (Figure S6c of Supporting Information). The high frequency band at 3442 cm−1 belongs to the hydroxyl group bridging between two metal ions. These spectral characteristics confirmed that the formation of MoOx nanoparticles. The bands at 3561 and 3324 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of –OH and N-H bonds of FA, respectively (Figure S6d of Supporting Information). Further, the bands at 2925 and 2838 cm−1 belong to the vibrational modes of CH2 group (asymmetric and symmetric). Noticeably, the band at 1696 cm−1 corresponds to the C = O group (stretching vibration). The bands at 1485 and 1412 cm−1 are ascribed to the presence of phenyl and pterin moieties of FA. It can be clearly seen that the peaks at 3425 cm−1 (–OH stretching), 1632 cm−1 (–C = O stretching), 857 and 823 cm−1 (mono − Mo-O stretching), 3400–3600 cm−1 (N-H group) were either slightly shifted or decreased the intensity (Figure S6e of Supporting Information), suggesting the surface modification of MoOx QDs with FA.

Fig. 2 represents the TEM images of FA-MoOx QDs at 2.0 and 5.0 scale bar, indicating the as-prepared FA-MoOx QDs are well dispersed. Fig. 2e shows the histogram of FA-MoOx QDs, confirming the mean size of 2.66 ± 1.20 nm, which revealed the synthesized FA-MoOx QDs are very small size. Inset of Fig. 2a shows that the prepared FA-MoOx QDs are in crystalline nature with an interlayer spacing of 0.251 nm, which is due to the (060) plane of MoOx QDs. The XPS was used to determine the elemental composition of FA-MoOx QDs. The full scan spectra of FA-MoOx QDs represents the presence of Mo N, C, and O elements in FA-MoOx QDs (Fig. 3 a). High resolution XPS spectra of Mo3d of FA-MoOx QDs showed the binding energies of 227.57, 229.91 and 233.01 eV, confirming the different oxidation states of Mo (Mo3+, Mo4+ and Mo6+) in FA-MoOx QDs (Fig. 3b). The high-resolution XPS of N1s XPS (Fig. 3c) reveals that the existence of N1s in asymmetrical pattern, indicating the presence of several distinct nitrogen oxidation states. The peaks at 398.1, 399.6 and 401.3 eV belong to the binding energies of N1s of FA-MoOx QDs, suggesting the existence of sp2 and sp hybridized N-atoms and –NH2 group on the surface of FA-MoOx QDs. Overall, the spectral characteristics demonstrated that the effective surface modification of MoOx QDs with FA. Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) was studied and the XRD pattern of FA-MoOx QDs are crystalline in nature (Figure S7 of Supporting Information). The peaks at 12.38°, 23.28° and 40.08° correspond to the as-preapred FA-MoOx QDs in orthorhombic phase [54–55].

In order to develop fluorescence “turn-off and on” assay, FA-MoOx QDs exhibited a fluorescence emission peak at 445 nm when excited at 360 nm (Fig. 1). First, the emission intensity of FA-MoOx QDs was investigated upon the addition of divalent metal ions (Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, and Hg2+, 1 mM). The emission of FA-MoOx QDs was selectively quenched (89%) in the presence of Cu2+ ion (Fig. 4 a), suggesting the formation of Cu2+ ion- FA-MoOx QDs. To restore the emission of FA-MoOx QDs, various biomolecules (ALP (200 U/L), adenosine triphosphate (ATP), adenosine monophosphate (AMP), cysteine, pyrophosphate ion (PPi), bovine serum albumin (BSA), trypsin, pepsin and glutathione, 500 µM) were separately introduced into the above solutions and the emission spectra of the above solutions were recorded (Fig. 4c). The emission intensity of FA-MoOx QDs at 445 nm was remarkably restored by the addition of ALP, revealing the FA-MoOx QDs could be used as a fluorescent “turn- off and on” probe for assaying of Cu2+ ion and ALP. To investigate the maximum quenching and restoring times, the fluorescence emission spectra of FA-MoOx QDs were studied upon the addition of Cu2+ ion and then ALP at different time intervals (30 – 240 s for Cu2+ ion and 15 – 900 s for ALP) (Figure S8a-b of Supporting Information). These results demonstrated that the maximum quenching of emission intensity of FA-MoOx QDs was achieved within 120 s whereas the fluorescence emission intensity of FA-MoOx QDs was restored maximum within 120 s, which confirsm the rapidty of the method for assaying of both Cu2+ ion and ALP in a single solution.

Before developing a calibration graph for assaying of ALP, the effect of Cu2+ ion concentration and pH were optimized (Figures S9–11 of Supporting Information). As shown in Figure S9 of Supporting Information, the fluorescence emission spectra of FA-MoOx QDs were studied with and without addition of Cu2+ ion at different Tris-HCl pH from 2.0 to 12.0. It can be noticed that the emission spectra did not cause any significant changes, suggesting the pH of solution did not effect on the quenching of FA-MoOx QDs by Cu2+ ion. The emission spectra of FA-MoOx QDs were investigated in the presence of ALP (50 U/L) using different concentrations of Cu2+ ion (1.0 – 2.5 mM), demonstrating 2.0 mM of Cu2+ ion was found to be optimum concentration for restoring of emission peak with the maximum intensity upon the introduction of ALP (Figures S10 of Supporting Information). It was noticed that pH of the solution plays a vital role in various enzymatic reactions, in view of this, the effect of pH was investigated on the restoring of emission peak of FA-MoOx QDs (Figure S11 of Supporting Information). To this, the fluorescence emission spectral characteristics of FA-MoOx QDs were studied by the addition of Cu2+ ion (2.0 mM) in the presence of ALP (50 U/L) at different Tris-HCl pH from 2.0 to 12.0. It can be noticed that the quenched emission intensity at 445 nm was steadily restored with increasing Tris-HCl pH from 2.0 to 9.0 and then there is a slight decrease in the intensity. Since, ALP has shown maximum activity at alkaline condition (pH 8.0–9.0) [56]. To support this, the quenched emission intensity was restored maximum at Tris-HCl pH 9.0, signifying Tris-HCl pH 9.0 was found to be optimum for assaying of ALP using FA-MoOx QDs as a probe.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism was investigated by using Stern–Volmer equation I0/I = 1 + Ksv[Q]. The Stern–Volmer plot exhibited a good linearity up to the concentration of Cu2+ ion (0.2–50 μM) (Figure S12a of Supporting Information). The quenching constant (Ksv) was calculated by using the Stern–Volmer equation which was observed at 3.1871 × 105 M−1 from the calibration graph. The higher value of Ksv can be suggested that the strong interaction between FA-MoOx QDs and Cu2+ ion. In addition, the average lifetime of FA-MoOx QDs is 13.27 ns, however, it was decreased to 9.90 ns after the addition of Cu2+ ion (Figure S5 of Supporting Information). This results demonstrated that the dynamic quenching is possibly due to the formation of the ground state of a non-fluorescent complex.

As depicted in Fig. 4a, FA-MoOx QDs display a strong emission intensity at 445 nm and the emission peak intensity was quenched significantly upon the addition of Cu2+ ion (1 mM). Interestingly, the addition of ALP into FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion, the emission intensity at 445 nm is restored just like as original emission spectra of FA-MoOx QDs. The UV–Visible spectra of FA-MoOx QDs, FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion and FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion-ALP are depicted in Figure S13 of Supporting Information. The absorption spectrum of FA-MoOx QDs showed two absorption bands at 312 and 362 nm, whereas the absorption spectrum of FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion exhibited only one band at 369 nm, confirming the interactions between FA-MoOx QDs and Cu2+ ion. However, upon the addition of ALP, the characteristic absorption bands of FA-MoOx QDs are successfully recovered, just as like FA-MoOx QDs (Figure S13 of Supporting Information). To understand the aggregation and dispersion states of FA-MoOx QDs in the presence of target analytes, TEM images of FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion and FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion-ALP were investigated and shown in Fig. 2c-d. It is clearly noticed that the well dispersion state of FA-MoOx QDs was changed to aggregation state upon the addition of Cu2+ ion, resulting to increase the average size of FA-MoOx QDs from 2.66 ± 1.20 to 5.01 ± 0.90 nm (Fig. 2f). Interestingly, the aggregated FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion system was significantly dispersed with the addition of ALP, favoring to reach the average size of 3.22 ± 1.01 nm (Fig. 2g). In addition, the average lifetime of FA-MoOx QDs is 13.27 ns, however it was decreased to 9.90 ns after the addition of Cu2+ ion (Figure S5 of Supporting Information). It is clearly evedent that the lifetime of FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion was remarkabley restored (13.14 ns) by introducing ALP, signifying the cleavage of FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion aggregated complex via ALP activity. FT-IR spectra of FA-MoOx QDs, FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion and FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion-ALP systems were studied and shown in Figure S14a-c of Supporting Information. It can be noticed atht the characteristics bands of FA-MoOx QDs at at 3425 cm−1 (–OH stretching), 1632 cm−1 (–C = O stretching), 857 and 823 cm−1 (mono − Mo-O stretching) are either shifted or drastically decreased the intensities after the addition of Cu2+ ion, interstingly, the spectral characteristics of FA-MoOx QDs are significantly reappeared by introducitng ALP into FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion sytesm. All these spectral studies confirmed that the quenching of FA-MoOx QDs emission intensity is due to the aggregation of FA-MoOx QDs induced by Cu2+ via FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion complex formation. After the addition of ALP into FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion complex, the interaction of Cu2+ ion was easily grabbed from FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion via ALP activity, resulting to restore the fluorescence of FA-MoOx QDs, which facilitates to develop fluorescence “turn- off and on” mechanism for assaying of Cu2+ ion and ALP (Scheme 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “turn- off and on” assays of Cu2+ ion and ALP
ce:section.ce:para: To study the sensitivity of FA-MoOx QDs as a fluorescent sensor for assaying of Cu2+ ion and ALP, intitially, the emission spectra of FA-MoOx QDs were investigated upon the addition of different concentratons of Cu2+ ion (0.20–500 μM), showing a gradual decrease in the emission intensity of FA-MoOx QDs with the increasing concentration of Cu2+ from (0.20–500 μM) (Fig. 5 a). As a result, a good linearity was noticed between the quenching emission intensity and concentration of Cu2+ ion from 0.2 to 100 μM (regression equation y = 0.6271x + 1.4717 (R2 = 0.9917)) (Figure S12a of Supporting Information). The limit of detection (LOD) is estimated as 29 nM by calculating 3σ/slope, where “σ” represents the standard deviation of the blank. Under the identical conditions, the fluorescence intensity of FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion were studied after the addition of different concentrations of ALP (0.06 – 150 U/L) (Fig. 5b). With increasing amounts of ALP, the emission intensity of FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion was systematically increased and a linear relationship was noticed between the ALP concentration range of 0.06 – 0.9 U/L and the emission intensity of FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion at 445 nm (Figure S12b of Supporting Information). The correlation coefficient (R2) is quite good (R2 = 0.9903), favoring to achieve a LOD of 0.026 U/L for ALP. The analytical performance of FA-MoOx QDs-based fluorescence assay is compared with the reported methods for sensing of ALP, exhibiting comparable or higher sensitivity than that of reported analytical strategies in the literature [38–39,45,56–63] shown in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of FA-MoOx QDs-based fluorescence assay
ce:section.ce:para: Usually, the selectivity is recognized as a key parameter for assaying of target analyte in real samples. For Cu2+ ion selectivity, the impacts of various interfering substances (metal ions – Cr3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+ and Hg2+ and anions - CH3COO–, HPO4 2-, PO4 3-, SO4 2- and CO3 2– and, 500 μM) were studied on the emission spectra of FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion with and without Cu2+ ion (Fig. 6 a). For ALP selectivity, the impacts of various interfering substances (biomolecules - lysozyme, adenine, guanine, cytosine, uracil and thymine; anions -HPO4 2-, PO4 3-, SO4 2- and CO3 2– and metal ions - Hg2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+, 500 μM) were studied on the fluorescence emission intensity of FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion with and without ALP (Fig. 6b). The addition of the above chemical substances did not show any obvious effect on the emission spectral characteristics of FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion-ALP, conforming the good selectivity of FA-MoOx QDs-Cu2+ ion system for assaying of ALP in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of ALP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the analytical performance of FA-MoOx QDs, the practical application of FA-MoOx QDs fluorescent sensor in real sample was investigated by quantifying the ALP levels in serum and urine samples. To this, the different concentrations of ALP (0.06, 0.07 and 0.08 U/L) were spiked into the human serum and urine sample and the concentration of ALP was determined by the developed method. The designed probe was evaluated by the determination of the recovery of spiked ALP in ten times diluted human serum and urine samples. As shown in Table S1 of Supporting Informatoin, (0.06, 0.07 and 0.08 U/L) of ALP were added into the serum and urine samples, respectively. The recovery of ALP was observed in the range of 96.25% −99.82%, with relative standard deviation (RSD) from 0.58% to 0.12%, which were satisfactory for performing of quantitative assay of ALP in biological samples. As shown in Table S1 of Supporting Information, the recoveries of ALP using FA-MoOx QDs-based fluorescence assay are in the range of 0.06 – 0.07 and of 96.25 – 99.82% with the RSD < 0.59% for serum and urine samples, revealing the FA-MoOx QDs-based fluorescence assay reliable, and could be used as a promising tool for assaying of ALP in real samples.

In summary, the fluorescence “turn- off and on” mechanism was established for assaying of Cu2+ ion and ALP using FA-MoOx QDs as a fluorescent probe. The as-prepared FA-MoOx QDs showed a strong blue fluorescence and a maximum emission intensity at 445 nm when excited them at 360 nm. A significant quenching of emission intensity of FA-MoOx QDs at 445 nm was notieced by introducing Cu2+ ion, interestingly, the quenched intensity at 445 nm was siginificantly restored upon the introduction of ALP, thereby offering fluorescence “turn- off and on” analytical strategy for sensing of Cu2+ ion and ALP, respectively. The developed FA-MoOx QDs-based fluorescence assay exhibited good selectivity and high sensitivity with impressive LODs of 29 nM and 0.026 U/L for Cu2+ ion and ALP, respectively. Importantly, FA-MoOx QDs-based analytical strategy was successfully applied to quantify ALP in serum and urine samples, showing good recoveries in the range of 96.25 – 99.82%, which indicates the developed method could be used for the quantification of ALP in biofluids and clinical samples. Overall, the surface modification of MoOx QDs with FA offers a facile and rapid analytical protocol for the selective assay of Cu2+ ion and ALP in real sample without aid of sophisticated analytical tools.

Mehul R. Kateshiya: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Software, Data curation, Validation, Formal analysis. Naved I. Malek: Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Suresh Kumar Kailasa: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India (EMR/2016/002621/IPC). MK sincerely acknowledges the Director, SVNIT, Surat, India for providing the doctoral fellowship to carry this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120659.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive detection for alkaline phosphatase using doped ZnS quantum dots with room temperature phosphorescence and its logic gate function

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots for an alkaline phosphatase assay and embryonic stem cell identification based on the inner filter effect

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Guo, X. Zhu, S. Wang, C. Lei, Y. Huang, Z. Nie, S. Yao, Fluorescent Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots for an alkaline phosphatase assay and embryonic stem cell identification based on the inner filter effect, Nanoscale. 10 (2018) 19579–19585.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A room-temperature phosphorescence sensor for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Li, J. Qin, Q. Xu, G. Yan, A room-temperature phosphorescence sensor for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots, Sensors Actuators B Chem. 274 (2018) 78–84.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Monitoring the activity and inhibition of alkaline phosphatase via quenching and restoration of the fluorescence of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Kang, Y. Ding, H. Zhou, Q. Liao, X. Yang, Y. Yang, J. Jiang, M. Yang, Monitoring the activity and inhibition of alkaline phosphatase via quenching and restoration of the fluorescence of carbon dots, Microchim. Acta. 182 (2015) 1161–1167.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorometric assay for alkaline phosphatase activity based on β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots through host-guest recognition

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Tang, Z. Qian, Y. Huang, J. Xu, H. Ao, M. Zhao, J. Zhou, J. Chen, H. Feng, A fluorometric assay for alkaline phosphatase activity based on β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots through host-guest recognition, Biosens. Bioelectron. 83 (2016) 274–280.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence detection of alkaline phosphatase activity with β-cyclodextrin-modified quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Jia, J.-P. Xu, D. Li, S.-P. Pang, Y. Fang, Z.-G. Song, J. Ji, Fluorescence detection of alkaline phosphatase activity with β-cyclodextrin-modified quantum dots, Chem. Commun. 46 (2010) 7166–7168.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A real-time fluorescent assay for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on carbon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.S. Qian, L.J. Chai, Y.Y. Huang, C. Tang, J. Jia Shen, J.R. Chen, H. Feng, A real-time fluorescent assay for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on carbon quantum dots, Biosens. Bioelectron. 68 (2015) 675–680.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemically generated green-fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots for facile monitoring alkaline phosphatase activity based on the Fe3+-mediating ON-OFF-ON-OFF fluorescence principle

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Niu, Y.L. Ying, X. Hua, Y. Niu, Y. Xu, Y.T. Long, Electrochemically generated green-fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots for facile monitoring alkaline phosphatase activity based on the Fe3+-mediating ON-OFF-ON-OFF fluorescence principle, Carbon 127 (2018) 340–348.
